Front: Need exactly one shared instance everywhere (config, logging, pool).
Back: Singleton — private ctor + static instance + getInstance(). JDK: Runtime.getRuntime(). Trade-off: global state, hard to test, thread-safety care.

Front: Superclass shouldn't know which concrete class to create; subclasses decide.
Back: Factory Method — abstract createX(); each subclass returns a concrete product. JDK: Calendar.getInstance(). Trade-off: one product type per factory.

Front: Create families of related objects that must be consistent.
Back: Abstract Factory — factory interface with several createX(); each concrete factory builds one family. JDK: DocumentBuilderFactory. Trade-off: new product changes every factory.

Front: Object with many optional fields; avoid telescoping constructors.
Back: Builder — chained setters then build() returns immutable object. JDK: StringBuilder. Trade-off: boilerplate.
Strategy — algorithm interface, concrete strategies, context delegates. JDK: Comparator in Collections.sort(). Trade-off: client must know strategies.

Front: One object changes; many others must be notified.
Back: Observer — subject holds observer list, calls update() on change. JDK: event listeners, Flow. Trade-off: order undefined, leak risk.

Front: Add behavior to an object dynamically without subclass explosion.
Back: Decorator — wrapper with same interface holds wrapped object. JDK: BufferedReader wrapping FileReader. Trade-off: many wrappers.

Front: Two incompatible interfaces must work together.
Back: Adapter — implements target interface, translates to adaptee. JDK: Arrays.asList, InputStreamReader. Trade-off: indirection; not for adding behavior.

Front: Complex subsystem needs one simple entry point.
Back: Facade — single class orchestrates the subsystem. JDK: SLF4J LoggerFactory. Trade-off: can become god-object.

Front: Stand-in that controls access to another object (lazy/remote/cache/auth).
Back: Proxy — same interface, adds control then delegates. JDK: java.lang.reflect.Proxy, Spring AOP. Trade-off: vs Decorator, Proxy controls access.

Front: Encapsulate a request as an object (queue, log, undo).
Back: Command — Command.execute(); holds receiver; invoker triggers. JDK: Runnable, Callable. Trade-off: class per command.
Template Method — final method sets order, abstract hooks overridden. JDK: AbstractList, InputStream.read(). Trade-off: inheritance, less flexible than Strategy.

Front: Treat individual objects and groups uniformly (trees).
Back: Composite — leaf and composite share interface; composite holds children. JDK: Swing Component/Container. Trade-off: type safety loosens.

Front: Traverse a collection without exposing internals.
Back: Iterator — hasNext()/next(). JDK: java.util.Iterator. Trade-off: concurrent modification.

Front: Behavior changes by internal state; avoid big if/switch.
Back: State — each state a class with common interface; context swaps state. Trade-off: vs Strategy, State transitions itself.
Factory Method = one product via inheritance. Abstract Factory = a family of products via composition.
Same structure. Strategy: client picks algorithm, no self-transition. State: object transitions itself between states.
Decorator adds behavior; Proxy controls access. Same wrapping structure.
Adapter changes the interface; Decorator keeps it and adds behavior.
